title: OLAP Warehouse: Google BigQuery
description: Serverless columnar data warehouse on Google BigQuery. No infrastructure to provision — BigQuery auto-scales compute on demand and charges per TB scanned (on-demand) or per reserved slot capacity (flat-rate). Separation of compute and storage is native; data lives in Colossus distributed storage. Particularly strong for ad-hoc analytics, ML integration via BigQuery ML, and organisations already on GCP. Query latency is 200ms-10s for typical analytical queries; simple aggregations with result caching can return in under 200ms. Hard limit of 300 concurrent interactive queries per project.

cost.costNotes: On-demand: $6.25/TB scanned. Flat-rate: 100 slots ~$2,000/mo, 500 slots ~$10,000/mo. Storage: $0.02/GB/mo active, $0.01/GB/mo long-term. Free tier: 1 TB queries/mo + 10 GB storage.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min ($0): free tier covers 1 TB/mo queries and 10 GB storage — sufficient for development. Max ($12,000): 500 reserved slots for high-concurrency dashboard workloads ($10,000/mo) + 50 TB active storage ($1,000/mo) + streaming ingestion costs. Most teams land $200-$2,000/mo on on-demand pricing.
provenance.adoptionCost: BigQuery is operationally simple (no cluster config). Adoption cost is schema design, dbt or Dataform pipeline setup, and Looker/Looker Studio integration. Estimate 10-15 developer days at ~$160/day = $2,000.
provenance.licenseCost: No software license. BigQuery is pay-as-you-go. GCP committed-use discounts available on flat-rate slots.

provides.reasoning: BigQuery delivers serverless MPP columnar query execution with automatic scaling and no cluster management.
Integrates natively with Looker, Looker Studio, Tableau, Power BI, and any JDBC-compatible BI tool via BigQuery JDBC driver.
Dataform, dbt-bigquery, and BigQuery Data Transfer Service enable ELT patterns with transformations running entirely inside BigQuery.

requires.reasoning: BigQuery expects data via batch load jobs, Datastream CDC, Pub/Sub streaming inserts, or Data Transfer Service. Direct high-frequency row inserts are expensive per streaming insert cost.
BigQuery uses Colossus distributed storage internally. External tables can query GCS directly without loading.

availability.reason: General architectural pattern applicable to any GCP OLAP workload

supports_nfr.reason: BigQuery p95 is 200ms-10s. Simple cached queries return in 200-500ms; complex aggregations over large datasets take 2-30s. BigQuery BI Engine can serve repeated dashboard queries in under 100ms.
BigQuery SLA is 99.99% for the query service. Serverless architecture with no cluster to manage provides high baseline availability.
BigQuery is serverless with no cluster restart. RTO is effectively 1-2 minutes for GCP zone-level issues; regional failover is transparent.
RPO is bounded by pipeline batch interval. BigQuery time-travel (7 days) enables point-in-time recovery.
BigQuery hard limit is 300 concurrent interactive queries per project. Above this, queries are queued or rejected with quota errors.
BigQuery streaming insert quota is 1 GB/s per project (~100 QPS for typical analytical row sizes). Above this, batch load jobs via GCS are required.
BigQuery uses GCP IAM for access control. Supports service account keys, Workload Identity Federation, and SSO via Google Workspace SAML.
BigQuery audit logs (data access, admin activity) export to Cloud Logging and Cloud Storage. Full query-level audit trail available.
BigQuery supports column-level security, dynamic data masking, and row-level access policies. Compatible with PII workloads.
Compatible with GDPR when data residency controls, column masking, and right-to-erasure pipeline procedures are in place.
Compatible with HIPAA when warehouse-level encryption and appropriate BAA coverage are configured.
Compatible with CCPA when consumer data deletion triggers a pipeline reprocess that excludes deleted records.
Compatible with SOX when query audit logs provide an immutable trail of data access and transformation operations.
Durability is handled by the underlying object storage layer (S3, GCS, ADLS). All major managed warehouses provide durable storage by default.

supports_constraints.reason: Google BigQuery is a GCP-native service.
BigQuery provides JDBC/ODBC drivers and is agnostic to language.
BigQuery serves as analytical backend for any platform.
BigQuery is selected specifically for OLAP analytical workloads.

pricing_model.description: on-demand: $6.25/TB scanned, no commitment — best for variable workloads. flat-rate: fixed slot capacity — best for predictable high-volume workloads. editions: BigQuery Editions (Standard/Enterprise/Enterprise+) — newer slot-based model with autoscaling.

ingestion_mode.description: batch-load-gcs: bulk load from GCS (free, highest throughput). datastream-cdc: continuous replication from OLTP (Postgres, MySQL, Oracle). pubsub-streaming: real-time streaming inserts via Pub/Sub. transfer-service: SaaS connectors (Salesforce, Google Ads, etc.).

query_result_cache.description: Caches results of repeated identical queries for 24 hours at no additional cost. Significantly reduces cost and latency for dashboard workloads.

bi_engine.description: In-memory analysis service for sub-second BI query response. Recommended for high-concurrency dashboard workloads with repeated queries over fixed datasets.

partitioning.description: Table partitioning reduces bytes scanned per query. date or timestamp partitioning on event_date or created_at columns is the most common and cost-effective strategy.
